subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Undertaking as Personal Obligation of the Lawyer
fact_pattern: In a real estate transaction, Lawyer Claude gives a written undertaking to the purchaser's lender that the mortgage funds will be used to discharge all existing encumbrances on the property. After closing, Claude's client (the vendor) refuses to authorize the discharge of one of the encumbrances, leaving it on title. Claude argues to the lender that he cannot fulfill the undertaking because his client refused to cooperate.
call_of_question: Is Claude's argument that he cannot perform the undertaking because his client refused to cooperate a valid defence to his obligation?

options.A: Yes — a lawyer's undertaking is only as binding as the client's instructions; if the client refuses to cooperate, the lawyer is released from the undertaking.
options.B: No — a lawyer's undertaking is the personal obligation of the lawyer giving it, not the client's obligation; Claude is bound to fulfill the undertaking regardless of whether his client cooperates.
options.C: Yes — the undertaking is void because it was conditional on the client's cooperation, which is an implied term.
options.D: No — but Claude is only obligated to make best efforts to perform; if the client refuses, best efforts satisfy the undertaking.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11, A lawyer's undertaking is a solemn personal commitment of the lawyer — it is not the client's promise. When a lawyer gives an undertaking, they are personally bound to fulfill it regardless of whether the client cooperates. The LSO's Commentary on undertakings and Rule 7.2-11 make clear that a lawyer must only give an undertaking they are personally able to honor. If fulfillment depends on client cooperation that cannot be guaranteed, the lawyer should not give the undertaking in the first place. Claude's failure to fulfill the undertaking is a breach of a personal professional obligation.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. An undertaking is not the client's promise — it is the lawyer's personal commitment. The lawyer cannot be released from an undertaking simply because the client refuses to cooperate.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — a lawyer's undertaking is the personal obligation of the lawyer giving it, not the client's obligation; Claude is bound to fulfill the undertaking regardless of whether his client cooperates.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Client cooperation is not an implied condition of a lawyer's undertaking. If the lawyer cannot guarantee performance independently of client cooperation, the undertaking should not be given.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. An undertaking is not a best-efforts obligation unless expressly stated. A clear undertaking to discharge encumbrances is an absolute commitment — best efforts do not satisfy it.
tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — undertaking is lawyer's personal obligation
client non-cooperation does not excuse non-performance
lawyer should not give undertaking unless able to honor

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Requirements for Giving Undertakings — Client vs. Solicitor Undertaking
fact_pattern: In a commercial transaction, opposing counsel asks Lawyer Dmitri to provide a solicitor's undertaking that his client will deliver a specific document within five business days of closing. Dmitri knows that producing the document depends on his client obtaining information from a third party over whom the client has no control. Dmitri's client tells him to give the undertaking anyway.
call_of_question: What should Dmitri do in accordance with Rule 7.2-11?

options.A: Dmitri should give the undertaking because his client has authorized it; client authorization is the only requirement.
options.B: Dmitri should give the undertaking and include a note that performance depends on a third party, which converts it from an absolute undertaking to a conditional undertaking.
options.C: Dmitri must not give the undertaking because he cannot personally guarantee performance; Rule 7.2-11 requires that a lawyer only give an undertaking they are personally able to honour; the appropriate mechanism is a client undertaking or covenant in the agreement, not a solicitor's undertaking.
options.D: Dmitri may give the undertaking and then seek to negotiate an extension once closing occurs if the document is not delivered in time.

explanation: Under Rule 7.2-11 requires a lawyer to give only those undertakings the lawyer is personally able to honor. Since delivery of the document depends on a third party over whom Dmitri's client has no control, Dmitri cannot personally guarantee performance. Giving the undertaking would expose Dmitri to professional misconduct liability if performance fails. The correct approach is to decline to give a solicitor's undertaking and instead have the client give a contractual covenant in the transaction documents, or make delivery conditional on the third party's cooperation with appropriate language.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Client authorization is not sufficient grounds to give an undertaking. Rule 7.2-11 requires that the lawyer be personally able to honor the undertaking, regardless of client instructions.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. Adding a note about third-party dependence may help clarify the obligation, but if the performance is truly beyond Dmitri's personal control, he should not give a solicitor's undertaking at all. The appropriate mechanism is a client covenant, not a conditional solicitor's undertaking.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Dmitri must not give the undertaking because he cannot personally guarantee performance; Rule 7.2-11 requires that a lawyer only give an undertaking they are personally able to honour; the appropriate mechanism is a client undertaking or covenant in the agreement, not a solicitor's undertaking.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Giving an undertaking with a plan to seek an extension later is not an acceptable approach. An undertaking must be performable at the time it is given — it is not appropriate to give an undertaking the lawyer expects may not be fulfilled.

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: giving undertakings — personally able to honour

tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — lawyer must only give undertaking they can personally honour
client undertaking vs. solicitor's undertaking
cannot give undertaking dependent on uncontrolled third party

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Breach of Undertaking — Professional Misconduct and Compensation
fact_pattern: Lawyer Elise gave a written undertaking to opposing counsel that she would deliver signed transfer documents by a specified date. Elise forgot about the undertaking. The deadline passed without delivery, causing the opposing party to incur additional legal costs and a delay fee from the lender. Elise argues that the breach was inadvertent and therefore does not constitute professional misconduct.
call_of_question: Is Elise's argument that inadvertent breach of an undertaking is not professional misconduct correct?

options.A: Yes — professional misconduct for breach of undertaking requires deliberate refusal to perform; inadvertent failure is merely a civil matter.
options.B: Yes — because the undertaking was not fulfilled due to oversight rather than bad faith, no professional responsibility consequence arises.
options.C: No — a lawyer who breaches an undertaking is generally subject to professional misconduct regardless of whether the breach was deliberate or inadvertent; a lawyer is required to fulfil their undertakings and must have adequate systems to ensure compliance; inadvertence is a mitigating factor, not a complete defence.
options.D: No — but liability for breach of an undertaking only extends to the additional legal costs incurred by opposing counsel, not to other consequential losses.

explanation: Under the Rules of Professional Conduct and well-established LSO disciplinary jurisprudence, failure to honor an undertaking constitutes professional misconduct regardless of whether the failure was deliberate or inadvertent. A lawyer is required to put in place adequate systems to track and honor undertakings. Inadvertence may be a mitigating factor in assessing the appropriate penalty, but it is not a complete defence to professional misconduct. Elise's argument is incorrect — she is professionally responsible for the breach and must take steps to remedy the consequences.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Professional misconduct for breach of undertaking does not require deliberate refusal. Inadvertent breach is also a breach, and adequate systems to track undertakings are required.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. Oversight or inadvertence does not shield a lawyer from professional misconduct for breach of undertaking. It may be a mitigating factor but not a complete defence.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — a lawyer who breaches an undertaking is generally subject to professional misconduct regardless of whether the breach was deliberate or inadvertent; a lawyer is required to fulfil their undertakings and must have adequate systems to ensure compliance; inadvertence is a mitigating factor, not a complete defence.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The scope of the lawyer's liability for consequences of a breach of undertaking is not limited to opposing counsel's legal costs — it may extend to all foreseeable consequences of the breach, and the professional misconduct exposure is separate from civil liability.
tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — breach of undertaking is professional misconduct
inadvertence is mitigating factor not complete defence
lawyer must have systems to track undertakings

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Trust Condition Scope — Client Instruction to Exceed Condition — Required Action
fact_pattern: Lawyer Fatima acts for the defendant in a civil proceeding. Opposing counsel, Lawyer Yusuf, sends Fatima a draft settlement agreement with a covering trust condition letter that reads: 'This draft is delivered to you in trust on the condition that it is to be shown to your client for approval purposes only and is not to be filed with any court, disclosed to any third party, or used for any purpose other than obtaining your client's instructions.' Fatima's client, after reviewing the draft, instructs Fatima to file the draft with the court as part of a motion record and also to provide a copy to a litigation funding company that is reviewing whether to fund the client's claim.
call_of_question: What must Fatima do in response to her client's instructions, given the trust condition under which the document was received?

options.A: Fatima must follow her client's instructions; once a document is in her possession and her client has directed her to use it, client autonomy overrides any restriction imposed by opposing counsel.
options.B: Fatima must comply with the trust condition and must not file the draft with the court or provide it to the litigation funder; the trust condition is a binding professional obligation on her personally from the moment she accepted the document, and client instructions cannot override it.
options.C: Fatima may file the draft with the court if she first seeks and obtains leave from the court; judicial authorization supersedes a trust condition imposed by a private lawyer.
options.D: Fatima should ask Yusuf to modify the trust condition; if Yusuf refuses, Fatima may proceed on her client's instructions because a trust condition that is overly restrictive cannot be enforced against a receiving lawyer.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — binding on receiving lawyer, A trust condition imposed by a delivering lawyer on documents or funds binds the receiving lawyer personally and immediately upon acceptance. Fatima accepted the draft settlement agreement subject to the trust condition that it was to be used only for obtaining client instructions and was not to be filed with any court or disclosed to third parties. That condition restricts the permissible use of the document absolutely within the stated scope. Fatima's client has now instructed her to use the document in ways that plainly exceed the trust condition — filing it with the court and sharing it with a litigation funder are both prohibited by the condition's terms. The critical point, and the Ethics Override Trap, is that client instruction cannot override the trust condition. The trust condition runs between Fatima personally and Yusuf as a professional obligation — it is not subject to modification by the client. If Fatima cannot comply with the condition (i.e., if her client's instructions make it impossible to proceed), her options are to seek Yusuf's agreement to vary the condition, or to withdraw from the file if client instructions are irreconcilable with the condition. She must not simply act on client direction in breach of the condition.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. This is an Ethics Override Trap — the paradigmatic wrong answer for trust condition questions. Client autonomy does not override a trust condition. The trust condition runs between the two lawyers professionally; the client is not a party to it and cannot waive it on Fatima's behalf.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Fatima must comply with the trust condition and must not file the draft with the court or provide it to the litigation funder; the trust condition is a binding professional obligation on her personally from the moment she accepted the document, and client instructions cannot override it.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. This is a Wrong Forum or Wrong Procedure Trap. Judicial authorization or court leave does not supersede a solicitor-to-solicitor trust condition. The trust condition is a professional obligation between lawyers — a court has no jurisdiction to relieve a lawyer from a binding trust condition simply because a party seeks to file the document.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. This is a Pragmatic Bluff. A receiving lawyer who is unwilling to be bound by a trust condition should not accept the document in the first place, or should return it immediately. A trust condition is not 'unenforceable' because it is restrictive — its restrictiveness is precisely the point. If Yusuf does not agree to vary it, Fatima is bound by it as accepted.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — binding on receiving lawyer; client instruction cannot override)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: trust conditions — scope of condition; client instruction to exceed scope; Ethics Override Trap

tested_concepts: trust condition is binding professional obligation on receiving lawyer personally upon acceptance
client instruction cannot override trust condition — Ethics Override Trap
using documents beyond trust condition scope is a professional responsibility breach
if unwilling to be bound by condition, must not accept the document or must return it

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: When Lawyer Must Not Give an Undertaking — Scope Not Within Personal Control
fact_pattern: In a residential real estate closing, the vendor's lawyer, Ingrid, is asked by the purchaser's lawyer to provide a solicitor's undertaking that the property will be free of all construction liens as of the closing date. Ingrid knows that a 45-day lien holdback period under the Construction Act has not yet expired and that there is a real possibility that unpaid subcontractors could file liens after closing. Ingrid's client tells her to give the undertaking to facilitate the closing.
call_of_question: Should Ingrid give the solicitor's undertaking as requested by the purchaser's lawyer?

options.A: Yes — client authorization is sufficient grounds to give a solicitor's undertaking; Ingrid's obligation is to follow her client's instructions.
options.B: Yes — providing a solicitor's undertaking is a standard real estate closing practice and Ingrid is obligated to give it to facilitate the transaction.
options.C: No — Rule 7.2-11 requires that a lawyer only give an undertaking the lawyer is personally capable of fulfilling; since lien rights arise under the Construction Act and belong to third parties over whom Ingrid has no control, she cannot personally guarantee that the property will be lien-free; she should not give the undertaking in its current form.
options.D: Yes — Ingrid may give the undertaking and if any liens are filed after closing, she can simply discharge them from the holdback funds.

explanation: Under Rule 7.2-11 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer to give only those undertakings the lawyer is personally able to honor. Construction lien rights under the Construction Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.30 belong to subcontractors and suppliers who are third parties entirely outside Ingrid's control. If any of them register a lien after closing, Ingrid cannot personally prevent it. Giving an undertaking that she cannot personally guarantee exposes her to professional misconduct liability. The appropriate mechanism is to negotiate a holdback arrangement or other protective mechanism in the transaction, not to give an unperformable undertaking.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Client authorization is not sufficient grounds to give an undertaking. Rule 7.2-11 requires that Ingrid be personally able to honor the undertaking, regardless of client instructions.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. While solicitor's undertakings are common in real estate, the rule requires that each undertaking be one the lawyer can personally perform. An undertaking to guarantee the absence of potential lien claims from third parties over whom Ingrid has no control is not one she can personally honor.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — Rule 7.2-11 requires that a lawyer only give an undertaking the lawyer is personally capable of fulfilling; since lien rights arise under the Construction Act and belong to third parties over whom Ingrid has no control, she cannot personally guarantee that the property will be lien-free; she should not give the undertaking in its current form.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The availability of holdback funds to discharge future liens does not make the undertaking performable by Ingrid personally — she still cannot guarantee the absence of liens as of closing. The plan to use holdbacks may inform the transaction structure but does not justify giving an unperformable undertaking.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (undertaking — personally capable of performance)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: undertaking — must not give when performance depends on uncontrolled third parties

tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — only give undertaking personally capable of honoring
cannot undertake regarding rights of uncontrolled third parties
client instruction does not authorize unperformable undertaking

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Consequences of Breach of Undertaking — Enforcement and LSO Discipline
fact_pattern: Lawyer Franco gave a written undertaking to opposing counsel in a commercial transaction that he would deliver executed share transfer documents within five business days of closing. The closing occurred. Franco's client provided the documents but they were signed with an incorrect date. Correcting the error required the client to re-execute the documents, which the client refused to do for six weeks, citing travel commitments. The undertaking expired without performance. Opposing counsel complains to the LSO.
call_of_question: How is Franco's breach of undertaking likely to be treated by the LSO, and what remedies does opposing counsel have?

options.A: The LSO is unlikely to take action because the breach was caused by the client's refusal to re-execute — client fault is a complete defence to a breach of undertaking complaint.
options.B: Breach of an undertaking is treated as professional misconduct regardless of the cause of non-performance; the fact that the client refused to cooperate does not excuse the breach because Franco's undertaking was his personal obligation; opposing counsel may also seek specific enforcement of the undertaking or damages in court.
options.C: The LSO will treat the breach as a civil dispute between the parties, not a professional responsibility matter, because the breach arose from a contractual performance issue.
options.D: Opposing counsel's only remedy is to sue Franco for breach of contract; the LSO does not deal with undertaking disputes.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (breach of undertaking — professional misconduct, A lawyer's undertaking is a personal professional obligation under Rule 7.2-11. The reason for non-performance — including client fault, client refusal, or third-party non-cooperation — does not excuse the breach. Franco should not have given the undertaking if he could not personally guarantee performance. The LSO treats breach of undertaking as professional misconduct, often regardless of whether the non-performance was deliberate. In addition to professional discipline, courts in Ontario have the power to enforce a lawyer's undertaking directly by ordering the lawyer to perform, or opposing counsel may sue for damages caused by the breach.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Client fault is not a complete defence to a breach of undertaking. An undertaking is the lawyer's personal obligation; the client's refusal is not a recognized defence because the lawyer should not have given an unperformable undertaking.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Breach of an undertaking is treated as professional misconduct regardless of the cause of non-performance; the fact that the client refused to cooperate does not excuse the breach because Franco's undertaking was his personal obligation; opposing counsel may also seek specific enforcement of the undertaking or damages in court.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Breach of undertaking is treated as a professional responsibility matter by the LSO, not merely as a civil contractual dispute. Professional misconduct proceedings may be taken in addition to any civil remedies.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Courts may enforce lawyer undertakings and the LSO does discipline lawyers for breach of undertaking. Opposing counsel is not limited to a civil breach of contract claim.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (breach of undertaking — professional misconduct; court enforcement)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: breach of undertaking — professional misconduct; client fault not a defence

tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — breach of undertaking is professional misconduct regardless of cause
client refusal is not a defence to breach of undertaking
courts may enforce undertaking; LSO may discipline for breach

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Reliance on Opposing Counsel's Undertaking — Professional Enforceability
fact_pattern: In a commercial real estate transaction, Lawyer Asha is acting for the purchaser. The vendor's lawyer, opposing counsel in the transaction, gives a written undertaking that the existing first mortgage on the property will be discharged within 10 business days of closing. Relying on this undertaking, Asha advises her client to close without receiving a discharge statement. Twelve days after closing, the mortgage has not been discharged. Asha's client asks whether the undertaking can be enforced and whether a court order is required.
call_of_question: Is a lawyer's undertaking enforceable as a professional commitment without requiring a separate court order, and what mechanisms are available when an undertaking is not honored?

options.A: No — a lawyer's undertaking is enforceable only if it is contained in a signed contract between the parties; without a contract, the undertaking has no legal force.
options.B: Yes — a lawyer's undertaking is enforceable as a personal professional commitment; the receiving lawyer may rely on the undertaking without requiring a court order; when the undertaking is not honored, the receiving lawyer may report the breach to the LSO and may seek court enforcement of the undertaking as a summary proceeding.
options.C: No — the receiving lawyer bears the risk of relying on an undertaking; if the other lawyer does not perform, the only remedy is a civil claim for damages.
options.D: Yes — but the undertaking can only be enforced by the LSO disciplinary process; courts have no jurisdiction to enforce a lawyer's undertaking directly.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 Commentary (reliance on undertaking, A lawyer's undertaking is a solemn personal commitment that is enforceable as a professional obligation without requiring a separate underlying contract. Courts in Ontario have recognized their inherent jurisdiction to summarily enforce lawyers' undertakings — a receiving lawyer can seek a court order compelling performance without going through the full civil litigation process. In addition, a breach of undertaking can be reported to the LSO as professional misconduct. The receiving lawyer is entitled to rely on the undertaking as a professional commitment and bears no obligation to demand additional security. Both avenues — LSO complaint and court enforcement — are available when an undertaking is not honored.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. A lawyer's undertaking does not require a formal contract to be enforceable. It is enforceable as a personal professional commitment by the giving lawyer, and courts recognize their inherent jurisdiction to enforce it summarily.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — a lawyer's undertaking is enforceable as a personal professional commitment; the receiving lawyer may rely on the undertaking without requiring a court order; when the undertaking is not honored, the receiving lawyer may report the breach to the LSO and may seek court enforcement of the undertaking as a summary proceeding.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The receiving lawyer does not bear the full risk of non-performance when relying on a professional undertaking. Court enforcement and LSO complaints are available. Civil damages may also be recoverable, but they are not the exclusive remedy.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Courts have inherent jurisdiction to enforce lawyers' undertakings directly — this is not limited to the LSO disciplinary process. Court enforcement and LSO proceedings are both available.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 Commentary (reliance on undertaking; court enforcement and LSO discipline)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: reliance on opposing counsel's undertaking — enforceability and remedies

tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 Commentary — undertaking enforceable without separate contract
courts have inherent jurisdiction to summarily enforce lawyer's undertaking
receiving lawyer may rely on undertaking without additional security

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Receiving Documents Subject to Trust Conditions — Must Comply or Return — Rule 7.2-11
fact_pattern: Lawyer Brett receives a package of confidential financial records from opposing counsel in a civil dispute, accompanied by a trust condition letter stating: 'These documents are produced to you on the condition that they will be used solely for the purposes of this litigation and will not be shared with any third parties or used for any collateral purpose.' Brett reviews the documents and realizes they contain highly useful information for a different matter he is handling for a related client. He decides to use the documents for that other matter while retaining them for the current litigation.
call_of_question: Is Brett's use of the documents for the collateral matter consistent with the trust condition imposed when the documents were delivered?

options.A: Yes — once documents are received by a lawyer in litigation, they may be used for any legitimate legal purpose; trust conditions are advisory only and cannot restrict how a receiving lawyer uses documents.
options.B: No — a trust condition received by a lawyer is a binding professional obligation; Brett must use the documents only for the purpose specified in the condition; using them for any other matter is a breach of the trust condition and may constitute professional misconduct.
options.C: Yes — as long as Brett keeps the documents confidential and does not share them externally, he may use them for related matters; the trust condition only restricts disclosure to third parties.
options.D: No — but Brett's only obligation is to return the documents once they are no longer needed for the current litigation; he may use them for the collateral matter in the interim.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — receiving documents — must comply or return), A trust condition received by a lawyer creates a binding professional obligation on the receiving lawyer to use the materials only in accordance with the terms of the condition. The condition here is clear: sole use for this litigation, no use for collateral purposes. Brett's use of the documents for a different matter directly violates the trust condition. If Brett is unwilling to be bound by the conditions, his obligation is to return the documents immediately — he cannot accept the documents while rejecting the conditions attached to them. Using the documents in violation of the trust condition is a breach of a professional obligation and may expose Brett to professional misconduct proceedings.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Trust conditions imposed by a sending lawyer on production of documents are not advisory — they are binding professional obligations on the receiving lawyer. A lawyer who accepts documents subject to conditions is bound by those conditions.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — a trust condition received by a lawyer is a binding professional obligation; Brett must use the documents only for the purpose specified in the condition; using them for any other matter is a breach of the trust condition and may constitute professional misconduct.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The trust condition prohibits use for any collateral purpose — not only disclosure to third parties. Using the documents internally for a different file is a collateral use prohibited by the condition, regardless of whether there is external disclosure.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Brett cannot use the documents for the collateral matter even temporarily. The trust condition attaches upon receipt and restricts use from that moment. If Brett does not agree to the conditions, he must return the documents immediately — not use them and return them later.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (trust conditions — receiving documents — must comply or return)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: trust conditions on documents — must comply with conditions or return documents immediately

tested_concepts: trust condition on documents is binding professional obligation on receiving lawyer
cannot use documents for purpose not specified in trust condition
must return documents if unwilling to comply with conditions

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Undertakings to Non-Lawyers — Same Binding Force — Rule 7.2-11
fact_pattern: Lawyer Chen is acting for a borrower in a mortgage refinancing. The lender is not represented by a lawyer and is a private individual. To facilitate the transaction, Chen gives a written undertaking directly to the private lender that he will register the new mortgage on title within five business days of receiving the mortgage funds. Chen registers the mortgage on day eight due to a processing delay at the land registry office. The private lender complains that the undertaking was not honored.
call_of_question: Does a lawyer's undertaking given directly to a non-lawyer (such as a private lender) carry the same binding professional force as one given to another lawyer?

options.A: No — the binding professional force of an undertaking depends on whether the recipient is a lawyer; undertakings given to non-lawyers are merely contractual promises with no special professional significance.
options.B: Yes — a lawyer's undertaking carries the same binding professional force regardless of whether the recipient is a lawyer or a non-lawyer; Chen is professionally bound to honor the undertaking given to the private lender and a breach carries the same consequences as a breach of an undertaking to a fellow lawyer.
options.C: No — undertakings to non-lawyers are enforceable only as contracts, not as professional obligations; the LSO has no jurisdiction over Chen's conduct toward the private lender.
options.D: Yes — but the only consequence of breach is that the private lender may sue Chen for breach of contract; no LSO proceedings arise from undertakings given to non-lawyers.

explanation: Under Rule 7.2-11 and the Commentary to the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct make clear that a lawyer's undertaking is a personal professional commitment regardless of whether it is given to another lawyer or to a non-lawyer such as a lender, registrar, or third party. The binding nature of the commitment and the professional misconduct consequences of breach are the same. A lawyer must only give undertakings to third parties that the lawyer is personally able to honor, and breach carries the same professional responsibility consequences. Chen's undertaking to the private lender is a professional commitment, and the breach — though caused by a registry delay — is a breach of a personal professional obligation.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The binding professional force of an undertaking does not depend on whether the recipient is a lawyer. Undertakings given to non-lawyers carry the same professional significance.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — a lawyer's undertaking carries the same binding professional force regardless of whether the recipient is a lawyer or a non-lawyer; Chen is professionally bound to honor the undertaking given to the private lender and a breach carries the same consequences as a breach of an undertaking to a fellow lawyer.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The LSO has jurisdiction over a lawyer's conduct toward all parties, including non-lawyers. Undertakings to non-lawyers are professional commitments subject to the same regulatory oversight.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Breach of an undertaking to a non-lawyer can give rise to LSO professional misconduct proceedings, not only civil liability. The professional character of the undertaking does not change based on the identity of the recipient.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (undertakings to non-lawyers — same professional force)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: undertaking to non-lawyer — same binding professional force as undertaking to lawyer

tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — undertaking to non-lawyers carries same professional binding force
LSO jurisdiction over undertakings to all parties not only lawyers
breach of undertaking to non-lawyer can result in professional misconduct proceedings

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Real Estate — Solicitor's Undertaking to Discharge Mortgage — Rule 7.2-11
fact_pattern: Lawyer Sandra acts for the vendor in a residential real estate closing. She gives a standard solicitor's undertaking to the purchaser's lawyer to discharge the existing mortgage on the property from the closing proceeds. Closing occurs and Sandra receives the full purchase price including sufficient funds to discharge the mortgage. However, she deposits the closing funds into the wrong trust account and does not process the mortgage discharge for three weeks, during which time the lender assesses an additional interest charge that reduces the available funds below the discharge amount. Sandra argues that the shortfall is the lender's fault for the interest calculation.
call_of_question: Is Sandra personally responsible for ensuring the mortgage discharge undertaking is honored, and does the lender's interest calculation excuse the shortfall?

options.A: No — Sandra has fulfilled her undertaking by receiving sufficient funds at closing; the subsequent interest charge is the lender's responsibility and releases Sandra from the undertaking.
options.B: Yes — Sandra's solicitor's undertaking to discharge the mortgage is her personal professional obligation; she must honor it regardless of the interest shortfall caused by her delay; she must make up any shortfall from her own resources if necessary, and the delay was caused by her error in depositing the funds incorrectly.
options.C: No — the undertaking becomes unenforceable when the lender's actions (charging interest) make it impossible to perform with the available funds; Sandra may discharge her obligation by returning the remaining funds to the purchaser.
options.D: Yes — Sandra is responsible, but only for the original amount of the mortgage balance at closing; interest charges accrued after closing are outside the scope of the undertaking.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (real estate — mortgage discharge undertaking — personal obligation), Sandra's solicitor's undertaking to discharge the mortgage is her personal professional commitment. She received sufficient funds at closing to perform the undertaking. The subsequent interest shortfall arose directly from her own error in depositing funds into the wrong trust account and delaying the discharge process for three weeks. A lawyer who causes the conditions that make an undertaking impossible to perform from available funds cannot escape professional responsibility by attributing the consequences to the other party's actions. Sandra must honor the undertaking, including making up any shortfall resulting from her own delay, and she must process the discharge promptly.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The fact that Sandra had sufficient funds at closing does not complete the undertaking — the undertaking is to actually discharge the mortgage. Her delay caused the shortfall, and she cannot shift that consequence to the lender.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — Sandra's solicitor's undertaking to discharge the mortgage is her personal professional obligation; she must honor it regardless of the interest shortfall caused by her delay; she must make up any shortfall from her own resources if necessary, and the delay was caused by her error in depositing the funds incorrectly.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The interest shortfall was caused by Sandra's own delay — it cannot be attributed to the lender's conduct so as to release Sandra from the undertaking. Sandra cannot discharge her obligation by returning the funds.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. A solicitor's undertaking to discharge a mortgage encompasses the full discharge obligation including any amounts owing at the time of discharge. It is not limited to the balance as of the closing date if the delay in discharge is caused by the undertaking lawyer.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (real estate — mortgage discharge undertaking — personal obligation)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: real estate — solicitor's undertaking to discharge mortgage — personal responsibility for performance

tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — solicitor's undertaking to discharge mortgage is personal professional commitment
lawyer who causes delay resulting in shortfall cannot attribute shortfall to lender
must honor undertaking even if requires making up shortfall from own resources

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Releasing Documents or Property in Reliance on Undertaking — LSO Enforcement
fact_pattern: In a civil dispute, Lawyer Faye acts for the plaintiff. In exchange for the plaintiff releasing original business records to the defendant's lawyer on a without-prejudice basis, opposing counsel gives Faye a written undertaking to return all original documents within 30 days regardless of the outcome of any negotiations. Thirty-five days later, the documents have not been returned and opposing counsel has stopped responding to Faye's requests. Faye asks her client how to enforce the undertaking.
call_of_question: What remedies are available to Faye when the opposing lawyer fails to honor a written undertaking to return documents?

options.A: Faye's only remedy is to commence a civil action against the opposing lawyer for breach of contract; the LSO does not handle disputes about documents between lawyers.
options.B: Faye may file a complaint with the LSO reporting the breach of undertaking, which is professional misconduct; she may also bring a summary court application to enforce the undertaking directly without requiring full civil litigation; additionally, damages may be recoverable if the client has suffered loss from the breach.
options.C: Faye should write a strongly worded letter to the opposing lawyer; if that does not work, she must wait until the civil proceedings are complete before seeking enforcement of the undertaking.
options.D: Faye may only seek enforcement through the LSO complaints process; courts have no jurisdiction to enforce undertakings between lawyers because they are professional obligations, not legal contracts.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (breach of undertaking — LSO complaint, court enforcement, damages), When an opposing lawyer fails to honor a written undertaking, the aggrieved party has multiple avenues of redress. First, the breach of undertaking is professional misconduct, and a complaint may be filed with the LSO. Second, Ontario courts have recognized their inherent jurisdiction to summarily enforce lawyers' undertakings — Faye can bring a court application directly without going through full civil litigation. This is a well-established remedy that allows rapid enforcement. Third, if the client has suffered damages from the breach (such as loss caused by inability to use the original documents), civil damages may be recoverable. All three remedies may be pursued simultaneously.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The LSO does handle breach of undertaking complaints — breach of undertaking is professional misconduct. Furthermore, courts may summarily enforce undertakings without full civil litigation being required.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Faye may file a complaint with the LSO reporting the breach of undertaking, which is professional misconduct; she may also bring a summary court application to enforce the undertaking directly without requiring full civil litigation; additionally, damages may be recoverable if the client has suffered loss from the breach.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Faye does not need to wait until the end of civil proceedings to enforce the undertaking. A summary court application for enforcement is available immediately and independently of the underlying civil dispute.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Courts do have jurisdiction to enforce lawyers' undertakings — this is a well-recognized aspect of courts' inherent jurisdiction over the legal profession. The LSO complaints process and court enforcement are both available and complement each other.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Undertakings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 7.2-11 (breach of undertaking — LSO complaint, court enforcement, damages)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: breach of undertaking — full range of remedies including LSO complaint and summary court enforcement

tested_concepts: Rule 7.2-11 — breach of undertaking is professional misconduct; complaint to LSO available
courts have inherent jurisdiction to summarily enforce lawyers' undertakings
LSO complaint and court enforcement are independent concurrent remedies
